ISLE Investigation

Applying the Atomic Model

Transfer · Cloud · Dielectric vs Conductor
Open with the hero image on the Van de Graaff. Yesterday we built the atomic model (orbit). Today we USE it — to explain rubbing, attraction of neutrals, and why metals and plastics behave differently.
Electrostatics
Bridge

From Model to Mechanism

Yesterday you built two things:

  • An atomic model — nucleus with protons and neutrons; electrons outside.
  • A commitment — which particle transfers when we rub two things together.

Today we test that model against things it needs to explain: rubbing, sticky balloons, and why metals attract harder than plastics.

30s orientation. Name the two anchors from Day 1 so the rest of this deck has somewhere to hang.
Whiteboard TaskISLE · Find a Pattern

Atom, +Ion, or −Ion?

For each atomic model, decide Atom, +Ion, or −Ion. Then determine its Mass Number.

A
B
C
D

Count the particles. Write your answers on your whiteboard.

Red = proton, gray = neutron, small blue = electron. Answers: A = Atom (A=7); B = +Ion (A=5); C = −Ion (A=3); D = −Ion (A=12). Make groups defend each. Reveal is next slide.
Reveal

Count the Charges

A
Atom
Mass Number = 7
B
+Ion
Mass Number = 5
C
−Ion
Mass Number = 3
D
−Ion
Mass Number = 12
Reflect

An ion isn't a broken atom — it's an atom with an imbalance. The protons never changed — only the electron count did.

Emphasize: protons are locked in. The only thing that can change to make an ion is electron count. This sets up the next question directly.
Commit PhaseISLE · Test an Idea

Which Particle Transfers?

When we rub two materials together and one becomes positive while the other becomes negative, something has to move between them.

Three candidates:

Proton Neutron Electron

Commit now. Which one is it — and why?

Students commit out loud or on whiteboards. Don't reveal — productive struggle. Ask groups to point to the part of the atom model that supports their answer.
Reveal

Electrons — and Here's the Proof

Protons and neutrons are locked in the nucleus. Electrons live outside — the only particles that can be knocked loose by rubbing.

If Protons Could Transfer…

Rubbing would turn carbon (pencil lead) into nitrogen (air) — it would transmute elements. We've never seen that happen.

If Neutrons Could Transfer…

Stability depends on neutron count. Rubbing a coin enough would make it radioactive. Nobody observes that either.

Reflect

The only particle whose transfer doesn't rewrite the periodic table is the electron. That's not a guess — it's what the evidence forces on us.

Don't just say "electrons transfer" — show WHY. Protons are identity. Neutrons are stability. Electrons are the only mobile piece. Two counterfactuals make it stick.
RepresentationISLE · Find a Pattern

Charge Diagrams: Zoom Out

So far you've drawn single atoms. A charge diagram zooms out to show a whole system. Draw a box for the object. Inside, draw a sample of its atoms and ions.

AAAA AAAA AAAA
Neutral System
vs.
AAAA AAAA ++++
Positively Charged System
A = neutral atom + = +ion − = −ion
New representational tool. Charge diagrams describe SYSTEMS not single atoms. Neutral = equal (or all atoms). Positively charged = more + than −.
Whiteboard TaskISLE · Test an Idea

Model It: Sweater & Balloon

On your 2×4 ft group whiteboard

Draw four charge diagrams:

  1. Sweater before rubbing
  2. Sweater after rubbing
  3. Balloon before rubbing
  4. Balloon after rubbing

Then use your diagrams to explain the attraction between sweater and balloon after rubbing.

5 minutes. Every group diagram gets photographed at the end.

Before diagrams = both full of A (neutral atoms). After diagrams = sweater gains +ions (lost electrons), balloon gains −ions (gained electrons). Walk around. Don't reveal until every group commits.
Reveal & Reflect

Sweater + Balloon: The Answer

BEFORE
AFTER
SWEATER
AAAA AAAA AA
AAAA AAA +++
BALLOON
AAAA AAAA AA
AAAA AAA −−−
Reflect

Sweater has a net + charge; balloon has a net − charge. Opposite charges attract — and the only thing that moved was a handful of electrons.

Protons count never changed. Only the electron distribution. Kids love this — it's clean and feels forensic.
Whiteboard Task

But Wait — What About Paper?

A charged balloon attracts a piece of unrubbed paper. The paper hasn't gained or lost any electrons. It's neutral.

Whiteboard it

Try to explain the attraction using only the orbit model (electrons on fixed rings). Draw what would have to happen.

Test your model. Does it work? Where does it get stuck?

Productive failure. Don't correct yet. Let them discover that the orbit model has no way to move electrons WITHIN an atom — rings don't stretch. This is the bridge to the cloud model.
Trap Alert

The Orbit Model Hits a Wall

In the orbit model, electrons live on fixed, rigid rings.

Rings don't stretch. Electrons on rings can't shift within an atom.

To explain the paper, we need a different model.

Reflect

The orbit model isn't wrong — it's just the wrong tool for this question. Every model has a zone where it works and a zone where it breaks.

Frame models as tools, not truth. Orbit stays useful for "which element" questions. Cloud takes over for "how does charge redistribute" questions.
Vocabulary Reveal

The Electron Cloud Model

Same atom. A different way to look at it.

The cloud model shows where electrons are likely to be — a fuzzy region of probability instead of a sharp ring.

Key Feature

Clouds Can Shift

The cloud can stretch or lean within an atom — without any electrons actually leaving. The nucleus stays put.

Orbit = snapshot. Cloud = long exposure. Different tools for different questions.

Don't say cloud is "better" than orbit. It answers different questions. Orbit: what element is this? Cloud: how does charge redistribute under influence?
PredictISLE · Test an Idea

Rod Approaches a Neutral Atom

A positively charged rod slowly approaches a single neutral atom drawn with the cloud model. Predict first — press Play to reveal.

Show paused. Groups commit a prediction on their 2×4 ft whiteboard: does the cloud shift? does the nucleus move? what's the net charge? Then press Play. Pause mid-shift and ask: "Is the atom an ion? Count the charges." Answer: no — still neutral. Name the effect next slide: polarization.
Reveal · Vocabulary

Polarization

Definition

Polarization

When a charged object is brought near a neutral atom, the atom's cloud shifts — one side becomes slightly more negative, the other side slightly more positive. The nucleus doesn't move. No electrons leave.

Reflect

The atom is still neutral — count the charges, they still cancel. But the near side is now close to "opposite" charge. That's the attraction.

Polarization is the big word for today. Emphasize: atom stays neutral overall. It's the GEOMETRY of the charge that changed. Near side pulls; far side pushes; net effect is attraction because near wins.
Whiteboard Task

Now Explain the Paper

On your 2×4 ft whiteboard

Sketch each piece, using the cloud model:

  • A charged balloon (mark its excess electrons).
  • A paper atom, drawn with its cloud.
  • Show how the paper atom's cloud polarizes when the balloon is near.
  • Label which side of the paper faces the balloon. Use your diagram to explain the attraction.

Success criteria: your explanation uses the words cloud, polarize, near side, and net neutral.

4 minutes. Target answer: balloon's near side is negative → paper cloud shifts AWAY from balloon → paper near side becomes + → + and − attract. Net paper charge still zero but geometry wins.
ObservationISLE · Observe

Two Rods Behave Differently

Trial 1 · Plastic

Balanced plastic rod. Charged rod approaches (no touch). Slow rotation toward it. Gentle attraction.

Trial 2 · Metal

Same setup, metal rod. Faster, farther rotation. Much stronger attraction.

Hint — what's inside each rod: Plastic = atoms only. Metal = atoms, +ions, AND free electrons (electrons not bound to any one atom).

Whiteboard it

Draw a charge diagram for each rod as the negative rod approaches. Use your diagrams to explain why the metal rotates more.

Run both trials live on the rotating stand if you can. Students see the behavior, then apply the composition hint on whiteboards. The vocabulary (dielectric / conductor) comes AFTER the explanation — don't name them yet.
Reveal

What Happens Inside

Plastic Rod

Tiny Shifts Only

No free electrons. Only the atom-clouds inside each atom polarize slightly. The + and − pockets are small and only on the near surface. Weak pull.

Metal Rod

Electrons Run Away

Free electrons stream all the way to the far end — away from the approaching negative rod. The entire near end becomes net positive. Strong pull.

Key contrast: in plastic, polarization is LOCAL (each atom shifts a tiny bit). In metal, electrons travel the FULL LENGTH. The distance the negative charge moves away is dramatically larger, so the near-side positive excess is dramatically larger, so the attraction is dramatically stronger.
Vocabulary Reveal

Dielectric vs Conductor

Conductor

Free electrons migrate

Some electrons are free — they hop through the lattice. Under a nearby negative rod, they pile up on the far side.

Examples: copper, aluminum, most metals, salt water.

Dielectric

Atoms polarize in place

Electrons stay bound to their own atom. Each cloud shifts a tiny bit; nuclei don't move. No long-range flow.

Examples: plastic, rubber, glass, paper, dry wood.

Play the animation. Pause mid-motion. Conductor side: electrons moved, nuclei didn't. Dielectric side: every cloud shifted a little, no electron left its atom. Vocabulary AFTER mechanism.
Diagnostic Task

Predict the Charge Diagrams

On your 2×4 ft whiteboard

Sketch the charge distribution inside each object below. Show + and − regions clearly.

  1. A charged rod held near a plastic block (dielectric).
  2. A charged rod held near a metal ball (neutral conductor).
  3. A charged rod touching the metal ball.

Success criteria: Your diagrams show where electrons can go in each material — and where they can't.

This is diagnostic — if groups can't do this, go back and reteach. Scenario 3 is the kicker: touching transfers real electrons and leaves the ball net negative, unlike the near-only cases where ball is still neutral overall.
🎭 Capstone · Embodied Assessment

Show What You Know

ISLE · Apply

Four short embodied activities — one for each concept. Groups rotate. I watch for accurate mechanism and correct vocabulary use.

Set up the capstone. 4 short theatrics, ~15 min total. This is where students demonstrate mastery through embodied action.
Theatrics · 4 Activities

Be the Physics

Activity 1 · 6 volunteers · 4 min

Be the Atom

Use your bodies to show the difference between an Atom, a +Ion, and a −Ion. Perform each state for the class.

Props: red cards (electrons), blue cards (protons).
Activity 2 · 8 volunteers · 5 min

Sweater & Balloon

Model the rubbing event with your bodies. Show the system before and after. Make clear which atoms become +ions and which become −ions.

Props: red cards (electrons) only. Protons are you.
Activity 3 · 6 volunteers · 4 min

Polarized People

Show what happens to a single neutral atom when a charged object approaches — without any electrons leaving.

Props: 1 red + 1 blue card per person. Teacher plays the charged rod.
Activity 4 · 12 volunteers · 5 min

Locked vs Loose

Two rows of 6. One models plastic, one models metal. Show how electrons behave in each when a charged rod approaches.

Props: red cards (electrons). Plastic row locks arms; metal row stands loose.
Don't pre-load setup mechanics — let groups figure out the staging. Walk around with the rubric: accurate mechanism, correct vocabulary, group coordination. Watch for students who move BODIES (wrong — that's nuclei) vs students who move CARDS (right — that's electrons). Correct vocab afterward, not mid-performance.
Exit EvidenceISLE · Apply

Which Model for Which Question?

Answer both. Justify why that model fits the question.

  1. Which model explains which element an atom is?   (Orbit / Cloud)
  2. Which model explains why a balloon sticks to your wall?   (Orbit / Cloud)
📤 Upload to Google Classroom · pick the format that shows what you know
📸
Photo
of your 2×4 ft group whiteboard
📝
Google Doc
typed reasoning
🎙️
Audio
60-second voice memo
✏️
Drawing
sketch both models, label the fit
Reveal & Reflect

Orbit is a counter — it tells you identity (protons → element). Cloud is a shape — it tells you how charge redistributes. The right model depends on what you're asking.

Orbit for identity (counting protons). Cloud for polarization (why neutral attracts charged). Students own both tools. UDL — accept any of the four upload formats. Reveal-and-reflect visible after submission; frame the answer aloud once everyone has turned in.
Electrostatics · Exit
